lines.text: Ladies and gentlemen and whatever the hell else survived the last two hours — welcome. You're listening to PRGE, it's {{CLOCK}}, and I'm Quinn Foley, which means either you're a loyal listener or you've exhausted every other option. Either way, I'm flattered.

[sound of glass pouring] That's Jameson. I say that every year for the new people. It's always Jameson. It will always be Jameson. I will die with Jameson in my blood and they'll put it on the headstone and people will say "at least he was consistent about one thing."

So. Two hours in. How's everyone doing? Show of hands. Actually don't raise your hands, keep them away from the windows. That's free survival advice from a man who is currently hiding in a location I will not disclose for reasons I'm sure some of you have very strong opinions about.
Let's get into it. You know what I love about Purge Night? And I mean this sincerely — it's the one night a year where the government is honest.

Three hundred and sixty-four days they pretend they care whether you live or die, and then one night they go "ah, you know what, have at it." That's not cruelty. That's clarity. I respect clarity. I was a politician. I know what dishonesty looks like because I did it professionally for eleven years.
I was good at it, too. Don't let anyone tell you otherwise. I was very good at lying to people who wanted to be lied to, which is — and I want to be clear about this — literally every voter.

You don't want the truth. Nobody wants the truth. You want someone to stand at a podium and say "things are going to be fine" while the building burns down behind them. That's democracy. That's the social contract.
Hobbes wrote about this — well, Hobbes wrote about a different thing, but the principle is the same. You surrender certain freedoms in exchange for the illusion that someone competent is in charge.
And nobody has been competent since — when? Name a year. Go ahead. I'll wait. I've got twelve hours and a bottle.
You can trace it, you know. The whole thing. People act like the Purge just appeared one day, poof, act of Congress, surprise. No. It was a hundred-year engineering project.

You go back to the 2020s — the Vance Succession, the dismantling of the — I'm not going to do the whole timeline, you've heard me do the timeline, I do it every year. If you want the timeline, pull up last year's broadcast. Tim archives everything. Tim, you archive everything, right?

[pause] Tim's nodding. Tim always nods. Tim is the most reliable man in Wisconsin and I say that with no exaggeration. I would trust Tim Pepinski with my life, which is saying something because I don't trust most people with my lunch order.

Anyway. Where was I. Right — the engineering of all this. My point is, when people say "how did we get here," the answer is you got here the same way you get anywhere: one bad decision at a time, made by people who were sure they were making good ones.
Kierkegaard said life can only be understood backwards but must be lived forwards. I'm paraphrasing. I'm also drinking. These things are related.
[sound of glass]
Let me tell you something about Madison specifically, because I know this city. I represented the 7th district for — well, you know how long.

And the thing about Madison is, it was always a contradiction. It was a progressive city inside a state that couldn't decide what it wanted to be, and the tension there — that's where the Purge hit hardest.
Because the people who thought they were safe? Educated, liberal, we-voted-the-right-way safe? They were the most blindsided. The East Side. The Near West. Willy Street.
These neighborhoods had yard signs. They had book clubs. And then one night a year the yard signs don't mean a goddamn thing.
That's not me being cynical. That's me being accurate. I know the difference. I went to Trinity College.
I also went to Uganda for a year but that was — that's a different — look, we're not doing that tonight.
[pause] We're not doing that tonight. I did it last year. I did the whole — the age of consent is different in every — no. No. Moving on.

The point is this. It's {{CLOCK}} on Purge Night and you're alive. You're listening to a pirate radio station run by a man who can't show his face in public and a DJ who's in the mob and a woman who — I love Marigold, I do, but that woman is one breath exercise away from starting a religion.
Don't tell her I said that. Actually, tell her. She'd like it.
And Tucker's out there somewhere. Tucker's out in it right now with a clipboard and a body camera and that flat voice doing math about who's dying and where.
God bless Tucker. They're braver than all of us and they'd hate me for saying that because bravery implies emotion and Tucker doesn't do emotion, Tucker does standard deviations.

So here we are. Your hosts for the apocalypse. A fugitive, a mobster, a cult leader, a statistician, a man who could fix a radio tower during a hurricane, and the most aggressively competitive married couple in the state of Wisconsin.
You're in good hands.
[glass] Terrible hands, actually. But they're the only hands available.
I'll be here until ten. Callers welcome. If you've got something to say, say it. If you've got something stupid to say, say it anyway, I've heard worse.
I once heard a man defend his voting record on live television. That man was me. Nothing you say tonight will be worse than that.
Let's take some calls.

tickerItems.text: Former 7th District representative Quinn Foley broadcasting from undisclosed location. Authorities have not commented.
Near West Side (Monroe St corridor) reports scattered activity 20:30–21:00. Lower than last year's pace.
Text from Middleton listener: 'Quinn please don't do the Uganda thing again this year.'
Willy Street book club reportedly barricaded inside Mystery To Me bookstore. Status: reading.
Text from Fitchburg listener: 'Foley's right about the yard signs. Ours didn't stop a single thing last year.'
The ones outside are not your responsibility.
